title: 萩への旅
eyebrow: A Japanese Lesson for Western Rural Japan
subtitle: Hagi, Yamaguchi · vocabulary, grammar & culture from a 145-year-old ryotei inn
footerText: Study aid built from takadai.co.jp. Target level N4 — JLPT badge marks words above it.

grammar.note: Verb-stem + たい means 'want to.' Adding んですが sets up context and softens the ask, inviting the listener to help — the single most useful travel opener.

ex.jp: 萩温泉に入りたいんですが、大浴場は何時までですか。
ex.en: I'd like to use the Hagi hot spring — until what time is the large bath open?

ex.jp: 見蘭牛のステーキを食べたいんですが、予約は必要ですか。
ex.en: I'd like to have the Kenran beef steak — do I need a reservation?

ex.jp: 世界遺産を見に行きたいんですが、バスセンターはどこですか。
ex.en: I'd like to go see the World Heritage sites — where is the bus terminal?

grammar.note: て-form + もいいですか. Your key for photos, an early check-in, or using a facility.

ex.jp: 写真を撮ってもいいですか。
少し早くチェックインしてもいいですか。
ex.en: May I check in a little early?

ex.jp: 貸切風呂を使ってもいいですか。
ex.en: May I use the private bath?

grammar.note: て-form + いただけますか. Politer than 〜てください — ideal for asking inn or shop staff to do something for you.

ex.jp: この料理を説明していただけますか。
明倫学舎までの道を教えていただけますか。
ex.en: Could you tell me the way to Meirin Gakusha?

ex.jp: おすすめの萩焼のお店を教えていただけますか。
ex.en: Could you recommend a Hagi-ware shop?

grammar.pat: 〜はありますか / 〜はどこですか
grammar.note: The workhorse for checking availability and locating things.

ex.jp: 地元の料理はありますか。
ex.en: Do you have any local dishes?

ex.jp: 素泊まりプランはありますか。
ex.en: Do you have a room-only plan?

ex.jp: 萩バスセンターはどこですか。
ex.en: Where is the Hagi Bus Center?

source.jp: 萩の中心地だから便利！
source.en: It's right in the middle of Hagi, so it's handy!

grammar.d: Means 'because / so.' The plain copula だ (= です) + から gives a reason. Attaches to a noun: 中心地 + だ + から = 'because it is the central area.' だから is the punchy, casual version for headlines; in polite speech use 中心地ですから.

source.jp: 旅の疲れを癒す萩温泉
source.en: the Hagi hot spring that soothes the fatigue of travel

grammar.d: A whole verb phrase — 旅の疲れを癒す — sits in front of 萩温泉 to describe it: 'travel-fatigue-soothing Hagi onsen.' Japanese places the modifier before the noun with no relative pronoun; the verb 癒す stays in plain form right before the noun.
旅の疲れ = 'the fatigue of travel' — の links two nouns like 'of.'

source.jp: 旬の素材を生かした、繊細な味を心ゆくまでご堪能ください。
source.en: Please savor to your heart's content the delicate flavors that make the most of seasonal ingredients.

grammar.d: A noun-modifying clause in the た-form: 旬の素材を生かした味 = 'flavors that have made the most of seasonal ingredients.' This た is a resultant-state descriptor, not past tense.
Honorific request: ご + a sino-Japanese noun + ください = a polished 'please do ~.' ご堪能ください = 'please savor (fully),' more elevated than everyday 〜てください.
Idiom: 'to one's heart's content' (lit. 心 heart + ゆく go + まで until).

vocab.m: to make the most of, to utilize

source.jp: 維新の故郷として多くの歴史的遺産を残す萩。
source.en: Hagi — which, as the birthplace of the Restoration, preserves many historical legacies.

grammar.d: Means 'as / in the role of.' 維新の故郷として = 'as the birthplace of the Restoration' — frames the capacity in which Hagi acts.
The entire phrase 維新の故郷として多くの歴史的遺産を残す modifies 萩: 'Hagi, which leaves behind many historical legacies.' Plain verb 残す sits directly before 萩.
Means 'many ~,' a more written/formal equivalent of たくさんの.

source.jp: 観光にもビジネスにもお選びいただいています。
source.en: Guests choose us for both sightseeing and business.

grammar.d: Means 'for both A and B.' Repeating に + も lists two situations inclusively: 観光にもビジネスにも = 'for sightseeing and for business alike.'

Humble honorific: お + verb-stem + いただく expresses that the business humbly receives the customer's action. お選びいただく = 'you kindly choose us.' With 〜ています it becomes an ongoing state. Classic service-industry keigo.

source.jp: 「基本は料理屋」の精神は明治11年の創業以来変わらず、地産地消のお料理をご提供しております。
source.en: The spirit of 'fundamentally a restaurant' has not changed since our founding in Meiji 11 (1878), and we continue to serve locally sourced cuisine.

grammar.d: 〜以来 means 'since ~' (a past starting point continuing to now). 変わらず is the negative conjunctive of 変わる — 'without changing.' Together: 'has not changed since founding.' This ず form is classical/literary; modern speech uses 変わらないで.

The full humble form: ご + verb-stem + しております (humble progressive of する). ご提供しております = 'we humbly continue to provide.' This is the gold standard of service-industry keigo — one level more formal than ご〜しています.

vocab.m: since, ever since (a point in the past)

source.jp: 豊かな自然に育まれた萩の幸を存分にお楽しみくださいませ。
source.en: Please enjoy to the full the bounty of Hagi, nurtured by its rich natural surroundings.

grammar.d: 育む (to nurture) in its passive form 育まれる = 'to be nurtured by.' The agent is 豊かな自然に (by rich nature). Japanese passive with に marks the cause or agent. Here it modifies 萩の幸: 'the bounty of Hagi that has been nurtured by rich nature.'

The most elevated form of a request: お + verb-stem + くださいませ. More formal than ご〜ください, and much more so than 〜てください. The ませ ending lifts it to written/ceremony register — typical of ryokan and traditional restaurant copy.
Adverb: 'to the full extent, as much as one likes.' More formal/literary than たくさん. Often paired with お楽しみください in hospitality contexts.

vocab.m: to the full, as much as one likes

source.jp: 竹と灯で作られた和やかな空間でお寛ぎください。
source.en: Please relax in the serene space created with bamboo and lantern light.

grammar.d: 作られた is the passive past of 作る (to make/create). 竹と灯で作られた空間 = 'a space that was created with bamboo and lantern light.' The で marks the material/means. Passive modifiers like this are everywhere in formal descriptive writing — learn to spot them before the noun.
The standard polite request: お + verb-stem + ください. お寛ぎください = 'please relax.' Verb 寛ぐ (くつろぐ) means to relax, unwind. The お prefix gives it a gentle, formal lift appropriate for hospitality.

source.jp: 古くから御贔屓にしていただいている文人の書画があり、小さな美術館のようです。
source.en: There are calligraphy works and paintings by literary figures who have long patronized us, making it feel like a small art museum.

grammar.p: 御贔屓にしていただく
grammar.d: 御贔屓 (ごひいき) means 'patronage, favour.' 御贔屓にする = 'to patronize, to be a loyal customer of.' In the form 御贔屓にしていただいている it becomes: 'who have graciously been giving us their patronage' — the business humbly receiving the customer's ongoing loyalty. A classic expression you'll see in traditional shops.
Means 'it seems like / it is like ~.' A simile/inference marker. 小さな美術館のようです = 'it is like a small art museum.' Softer than 〜です alone — the inn is being modest about its own cultural richness.

vocab.m: long ago, for a long time (adv. use of 古い)

source.jp: 萩ならではの大広間でのお食事は、幕末～明治時代を体感できます。
source.en: Dining in the grand banquet hall — something you can only find in Hagi — lets you feel the atmosphere of the late Edo through Meiji eras.

grammar.d: A fixed expression meaning 'only possible with / unique to ~.' 萩ならでは = 'something that could only be found in Hagi / uniquely Hagi.' It always follows a noun and implies no other place or thing could offer this. You'll see 〜ならではの + noun everywhere in regional tourism copy.

体感する = 'to experience physically, to feel in one's body' (stronger than just 感じる). With できます: 'you can physically experience ~.' This construction (noun + を + できます verb) is your standard 'you can do X here' phrasing for tourism settings.

vocab.m: unique to, only possible with (set expression)
end of the Edo shogunate period
to feel physically, to experience firsthand

culture.body: Takadai is a 料亭 (ryōtei) — a traditional fine-dining restaurant — that also runs as an inn, founded in 1878. In the countryside, longevity is the brand: expect a founding year on the sign (創業), the word 老舗 for a long-established house, framed calligraphy from a famous past guest, and a strong sense of おもてなし (hospitality).

culture.en: Local land & sea on the plate
culture.body: The kitchen leans on 地産地消 — sourcing from the immediate region. Look for 見蘭牛 (Kenran beef), Hagi's marbled brand beef, and Sea-of-Japan seafood: お刺身, 鯛のあら炊き (simmered sea-bream collar), and 串揚げ. Meals are usually 夕朝食付き; the budget option is 素泊まり.

Takadai opened はぎ温泉 in 2008, with a 大浴場 (large bath) and a free 貸切風呂 (private bath). Wash and rinse fully before entering the shared tub — no swimwear, small towel never touches the water. Tattoos? Ask first, or just book the 貸切風呂.

Hagi was the 城下町 of the Chōshū domain and the launch pad for the 明治維新. Because the capital moved away in 1863, the Edo-era streets survived intact; several sites became 世界遺産 in 2015. Look for 松下村塾, the 萩城跡 castle ruins, and the prized 萩焼 pottery — a classic souvenir.
